title: Draba bosniaca
intro: Draba bosniaca, also known as Bosnian whitlowgrass, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Brassicaceae family, characterized by its small, white or pink flowers and narrow, grass-like leaves. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including respiratory problems, such as bronchitis and asthma, due to its expectorant properties and ability to reduce inflammation. The bioactive constituents of Draba bosniaca include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Draba bosniaca is often prepared as a tea or tincture, and is also used in the production of various extracts and capsules to support respiratory health and alleviate symptoms of colds and flu.
taxonomy: Draba bosniaca is classified as a species within the genus Draba, which belongs to the family Brassicaceae. The genus Draba is a group of small, annual or perennial flowering plants often referred to as whitlowgrasses or false whitlowgrasses. The family Brassicaceae is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as mustard or cabbage family. These plants are native to the Northern Hemisphere, including parts of Europe and Asia. Draba bosniaca is a specific species within this larger classification.

side_effects: Draba bosniaca, also known as Bosnia's whitlowgrass, is a plant that may cause adverse effects when ingested or handled improperly. Ingestion of the plant can lead to gastrointestinal issues, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Skin contact with the plant's sap may cause irritation, redness, and itching.
